name: McCoy Tyner
abstract: Alfred McCoy Tyner (December 11, 1938 – March 6, 2020) was an American jazz pianist and composer known for his work with the John Coltrane Quartet from 1960 to 1965 and his long solo career afterward. He was an NEA Jazz Master and a five-time Grammy Award winner.

works_created.t: You Taught My Heart to Sing

about_quotes.speaker: Marilyn Crispell
about_quotes.evidence: And McCoy Tyner, who is actually probably the first one because of listening to “A Love Supreme.” So he was a huge influence and for a long time I think my playing was very influenced by him
about_quotes.doc_title: Marilyn Crispell interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, January 21, 2022

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Owens
about_quotes.evidence: I went back to Japan a little later in that year with a special drum tour that had Art Blakey, Elvin Jones, Tony Williams, McCoy Tyner, Ben Tucker played bass, Wayne Shorter and myself.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jimmy Owens interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 12, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Art Farmer
about_quotes.evidence: we wound up using my brother and Dave Bailey, and then Benny said, 'There’s a young pianist over in Philadelphia... It was McCoy Tyner, so we got him.' ... McCoy Tyner was the pianist
about_quotes.doc_title: Art Farmer — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Chick Corea
about_quotes.evidence: Think about one of my all-time favorite social-musical groups of all time, which is the John Coltrane Quartet with McCoy Tyner, Jimmy Garrison, and Elvin Jones.
about_quotes.doc_title: Chick Corea — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Owens
about_quotes.evidence: I went again later on in 1966 with a drum tour. It was Tony Williams, Elvin Jones, Art Blakey, McCoy Tyner, Ben Tucker on bass, Wayne Shorter and myself.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jimmy Owens — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Tom Lellis
about_quotes.evidence: The first one was McCoy Tyner and Chick Corea... those were specifically the influences that catapulted me to try to play piano.
about_quotes.doc_title: Tom Lellis interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, July 31, 2007
